From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), personal bravery, achievement, or service are rewarded with honours. The honours system consists of three types of award:

* Honours are used to recognise merit in terms of achievement and service;
* [Decorations](/wiki/State_decoration "State decoration") tend to be used to recognise specific deeds;
* [Medals](/wiki/Medal "Medal") are used to recognise service on a particular operation or in a specific theatre, long or valuable service, and good conduct.

Appointments to the various [orders](/wiki/Order_(distinction) "Order (distinction)") and awards of other honours are usually published in *[The London Gazette](/wiki/The_London_Gazette "The London Gazette")*.

## Brief history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Brief history")]

Although the [Anglo-Saxon monarchs](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_monarchs "Anglo-Saxon monarchs") are known to have rewarded their loyal subjects with rings and other symbols of favour, it was the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") who introduced [knighthoods](/wiki/Knighthood "Knighthood") as part of their [feudal government](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"). The first English [order of chivalry](/wiki/Order_of_chivalry "Order of chivalry"), the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"), was created in 1348 by [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III"). Since then, the system has evolved to address the changing need to recognise other forms of service to the United Kingdom.

## System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: System")]

As the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), the [Sovereign](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") is the [fount of honour](/wiki/Fount_of_honour "Fount of honour"),[[1]](#cite_note-BrMonHonours-1) but the system for identifying and recognising candidates to honour has changed considerably over time. Various orders of knighthood have been created (see below) as well as awards for military service, bravery, merit, and achievement which take the form of decorations or medals. Most medals are not graded. Each one recognises specific service so there are normally set criteria which must be met. These criteria may include a period of time and will often delimit a particular geographic region. Medals are not normally presented by the Sovereign. A full list is printed in the "order of wear", published (infrequently) by the *[London Gazette](/wiki/London_Gazette "London Gazette")*.

### Orders of honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Orders of honours")]

Honours are split into classes ("orders") and are graded to distinguish different degrees of achievement or service, according to various criteria.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Nominations are reviewed by [honours committees](/wiki/Honours_committee "Honours committee") made up of government officials and private citizens from different fields, who meet twice a year to discuss the candidates and make recommendations for appropriate honours to be awarded by the Sovereign.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Selection and nomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Selection and nomination")]

#### New Year and Birthday honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Year and Birthday honours")]

Main articles: [New Year Honours](/wiki/New_Year_Honours "New Year Honours") and [Birthday Honours](/wiki/Birthday_Honours "Birthday Honours")

A list of approximately 1,350 names is published twice a year, at the [New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year") and on the date of the [Sovereign's (official) birthday](/wiki/King%27s_Official_Birthday "King's Official Birthday"). Since decisions are inevitably subjective, the twice-yearly honours lists often provoke criticism from those who feel strongly about particular cases.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Candidates are identified by public or private bodies, by government departments, or are nominated by members of the public. Depending on their roles, those people selected by the honours committee are submitted either to the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), [Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs](/wiki/Foreign_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Foreign Secretary (United Kingdom)"), or [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence") for their approval before being sent to the Sovereign for final approval. Certain honours are conferred solely at the Sovereign's discretion, such as appointments to the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"),[[6]](#cite_note-BrMonGarter-6) the [Order of the Thistle](/wiki/Order_of_the_Thistle "Order of the Thistle"), the [Royal Victorian Order](/wiki/Royal_Victorian_Order "Royal Victorian Order"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the [Order of Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Merit "Order of Merit").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The honours' insignia are then presented by the Sovereign or his representative at investitures held at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle") or the [Palace of Holyroodhouse](/wiki/Holyrood_Palace "Holyrood Palace"); [Prince Charles](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), [Prince William](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales") and [Princess Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Princess_Royal "Anne, Princess Royal") deputised for [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") with William and Anne continuing to do so for [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") since his September 2022 accession.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

#### Prime Minister's Resignation Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Prime Minister's Resignation Honours")]

By convention, a departing [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") is allowed to nominate [Prime Minister's Resignation Honours](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Resignation_Honours "Prime Minister's Resignation Honours"), to reward political and personal service. In recent history, only [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") and [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") have not taken up this privilege (although Brown did issue the [2010 Dissolution Honours](/wiki/2010_Dissolution_Honours "2010 Dissolution Honours")).

#### Special Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Special Honours")]

As part of the British honours system, Special Honours are issued at the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarch_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarch of the United Kingdom")'s [pleasure](/wiki/At_His_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At His Majesty's pleasure") at any given time. The Special Honours refer to the awards made within royal prerogative, operational honours, political honours and other honours awarded outside the [New Years Honours](/wiki/New_Years_Honours "New Years Honours") and [Birthday Honours](/wiki/Birthday_Honours "Birthday Honours").

#### Crown Honours Lists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Crown Honours Lists")]

Main article: [Crown Honours Lists](/wiki/Crown_Honours_Lists "Crown Honours Lists")

## Orders of chivalry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Orders of chivalry")]

See also: [Order of chivalry](/wiki/Order_of_chivalry "Order of chivalry")

### Current orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Current orders")]

The current system is made up of six orders of chivalry and four orders of merit. The [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute") of each order specify matters such as the size of the order, the use of [post-nominal letters](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") and insignia design and display. These are ordered by the order of wear.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

### Dormant orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dormant orders")]

Orders were created for particular reasons at particular times. In some cases these reasons have ceased to have any validity and orders have fallen into [abeyance](/wiki/Abeyance "Abeyance"), primarily due to the decline of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") during the twentieth century. Reforms of the system have sometimes made other changes. For example, the British Empire Medal temporarily ceased to be awarded in the UK in 1993, as was the companion level award of the [Imperial Service Order](/wiki/Imperial_Service_Order "Imperial Service Order") (although its medal is still used). The British Empire Medal was revived, however, in 2012 with 293 BEMs awarded for the [2012 Birthday Honours](/wiki/2012_Birthday_Honours "2012 Birthday Honours"), and has continued to be awarded in some other Commonwealth nations.

The Order of St Patrick was founded in 1783 by [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") for the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), and after the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") continued for Irish peers in the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). After the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State")'s secession in 1922, only members of the royal family were appointed to the order, the last in 1936. The last surviving knight was [Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester"), who died on 10 June 1974. Although dormant, the order technically still exists, and may be used as an award at any time.

Orders relating to the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") or the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire") are also dormant. The senior order, the Order of the Star of India, was divided into three grades, Knight Grand Commander, Knight Commander and Companion, of which the first and highest was conferred upon the Princes and Chiefs of Indian states and upon important British [civil servants](/wiki/Civil_servant "Civil servant") working in India. Women were not eligible to receive the award. The junior order, the Order of the Indian Empire, was divided into the same ranks and also excluded women. The third order, the Order of the Crown of India, was used exclusively to honour women. Its members, all sharing a single grade, consisted of the wives and close female relatives of Indian Princes or Chiefs; the [Viceroy](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") or [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India"); the Governors of [Bombay](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Bombay_Presidency "List of governors of Bombay Presidency"), [Madras](/wiki/List_of_colonial_Governors_and_Presidents_of_Madras_Presidency "List of colonial Governors and Presidents of Madras Presidency") and [Bengal](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Bengal_Presidency "List of governors of Bengal Presidency"); the [Principal Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India"); and the [Commander-in-Chief in India](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief,_India "Commander-in-Chief, India"). Upon [Indian independence](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, appointments to all these orders ceased.

HH [Maharaja Tej Singh Prabhakar Bahadur of Alwar](/wiki/Tej_Singh_Prabhakar_Bahadur "Tej Singh Prabhakar Bahadur"), who was a KCSI and the last surviving member of the [Order of the Star of India](/wiki/Order_of_the_Star_of_India "Order of the Star of India"), died in February 2009, aged 97. The last surviving member of the [Order of the Indian Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_Indian_Empire "Order of the Indian Empire"), HH [Maharaja Meghrajji III of Dhrangadhra-Halvad](/wiki/Meghrajji_III_of_Dhrangadhra-Halvad "Meghrajji III of Dhrangadhra-Halvad"), a KCIE died in August 2010, aged 87. Queen Elizabeth II was appointed to the [Order of the Crown of India](/wiki/Order_of_the_Crown_of_India "Order of the Crown of India") (then as Princess Elizabeth) and was the last surviving former member of that order until her death in September 2022, aged 96.

The [Order of Burma](/wiki/Order_of_Burma "Order of Burma") was created in May 1940 by King [George VI of the United Kingdom](/wiki/George_VI_of_the_United_Kingdom "George VI of the United Kingdom") to recognise subjects of the British colony of [Burma](/wiki/British_Burma "British Burma") ([Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")) after it became a distinct colony, separate from [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"). This order had one class which entitled the member to the postnominal letters OB but no title. It was originally intended to reward long and faithful service by military and police. In 1945 the Royal Warrant was altered to allow for membership for acts of gallantry as well as meritorious service. The Order was one of the rarest awarded with only 33 appointments by the time appointments were discontinued in 1948 when Burma declared independence.

The [Royal Guelphic Order](/wiki/Royal_Guelphic_Order "Royal Guelphic Order"), also known as the Hanoverian Guelphic Order, was an honour founded by [George, Prince Regent](/wiki/George_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "George IV of the United Kingdom") in the name of his father [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") in 1815. In the United Kingdom, it was used only briefly, until the death of [William IV](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") in 1837. That is when the [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with the [Kingdom of Hanover](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover") ended due to the Hanover throne succession, which followed [Salic Law](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law"), unlike the United Kingdom, where women could inherit the throne. The order continued for some time as a national order of Hanover, until the Kingdom of Hanover’s defeat against [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") and forced dissolution in 1866. Since then, it has been a [house order](/wiki/Dynastic_order_of_knighthood "Dynastic order of knighthood") to be awarded by the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover"). The order's current head is [Ernst August Prinz von Hanover](/wiki/Ernst_August,_Prince_of_Hanover_(born_1954) "Ernst August, Prince of Hanover (born 1954)"), head of the House of Hanover. The Order includes two Divisions: Civil and Military. During the personal union of the United Kingdom and Hanover, it originally had three classes. Today, after several reorganizations since 1841, it is a house order with four classes and an additional Cross of Merit.

## Other honours and appointments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other honours and appointments")]

### Hereditary peerage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Hereditary peerage")]

There are five ranks of hereditary [peerage](/wiki/Peerage_of_the_United_Kingdom "Peerage of the United Kingdom"): [duke](/wiki/Duke "Duke"), [marquess](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess"), [earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl"), [viscount](/wiki/Viscount "Viscount"), and [baron](/wiki/Baron "Baron"). Until the mid-20th century, peerages were usually [hereditary](/wiki/Hereditary_peer "Hereditary peer"). Until the end of the 20th century, English, Scottish, British, and UK peerages (except, until very recent times, those for the time being held by women) carried the right to a seat in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords").

Hereditary peerages are now normally given only to [Royal Family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family") members. The most recent were the grants to: Queen Elizabeth II's youngest son, [Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh"), on his marriage in 1999; the Queen's grandson [Prince William](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"), made the [Duke of Cambridge](/wiki/Duke_of_Cambridge "Duke of Cambridge") on the morning before his marriage to [Catherine Middleton](/wiki/Catherine,_Princess_of_Wales "Catherine, Princess of Wales") on 29 April 2011; and the Queen's grandson [Prince Harry](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex"), made the [Duke of Sussex](/wiki/Duke_of_Sussex "Duke of Sussex") on the morning before his marriage to [Meghan Markle](/wiki/Meghan,_Duchess_of_Sussex "Meghan, Duchess of Sussex") on 19 May 2018. No hereditary peerages were granted to [commoners](/wiki/Commoner "Commoner") after the Labour Party came to power in 1964, until [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") tentatively reintroduced them by two grants to men with no sons in 1983: Speaker of the House of Commons [George Thomas](/wiki/George_Thomas,_1st_Viscount_Tonypandy "George Thomas, 1st Viscount Tonypandy") and former deputy prime minister [William Whitelaw](/wiki/William_Whitelaw,_1st_Viscount_Whitelaw "William Whitelaw, 1st Viscount Whitelaw"). Both titles died with their holders. She followed this with an earldom in 1984 for former prime minister [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") not long before his death, reviving a traditional honour for former prime ministers. Macmillan's grandson succeeded him on his death in 1986. No hereditary peerages have been created since, and Thatcher's own title was a life peerage (see further explanation below). The concession of a [baronetcy](/wiki/Baronet "Baronet") (i.e., hereditary knighthood), was granted to her husband Denis following her resignation (explained below).

Hereditary peerages are not "honours under the crown" and cannot normally be withdrawn. A peerage can be revoked only by a specific Act of Parliament, and then only for the current holder, in the case of hereditary peerages. A hereditary peer can disclaim his peerage for his own lifetime under [Peerage Act 1963](/wiki/Peerage_Act_1963 "Peerage Act 1963") within a year of inheriting the title.

### Life peerage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Life peerage")]

Modern [life peerages](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer") were introduced under the [Appellate Jurisdiction Act 1876](/wiki/Appellate_Jurisdiction_Act_1876 "Appellate Jurisdiction Act 1876"), following a [test case](/wiki/Test_case_(law) "Test case (law)"), the [*Wensleydale Peerage Case*](/wiki/Peerage_law#Wensleydale_Peerage_Case_(1856) "Peerage law") (1856), which established that non-statutory life peers would not have the right to sit in the House of Lords. At that time, life peerages were intended only for [law lords](/wiki/Lords_of_Appeal_in_Ordinary "Lords of Appeal in Ordinary"), who would introduce appellate legal expertise into the chamber without conferring rights on future generations, which might not have legal experts.

Subsequently, under the [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958"), life peerages became the norm for all new grants outside the Royal Family. This was viewed as a modest reform of the second legislative chamber. However, its effects were gradual because hereditary peers and their successors retained their rights to attend and vote with the life peers. All hereditary peers, except 92 chosen in a secret ballot of all hereditary peers, have now lost their rights to sit in the second chamber. All hereditary peers, however, retain dining rights to the House of Lords, viewed as "the best club in London".

All life peers hold the rank of [baron](/wiki/Baron "Baron") and automatically have the right to sit in the House of Lords. The title exists only for the duration of their own lifetime and is not passed to their heirs, although the children of life peers enjoy the same [courtesy titles](/wiki/Courtesy_titles_in_the_United_Kingdom "Courtesy titles in the United Kingdom") as those of hereditary peers. Some life peerages are created as an honour for achievement, some for the specific purpose of introducing legislators from the various political parties (known as [working peers](/wiki/Working_peers "Working peers")), and some under the Appellate Jurisdiction Act 1876, with a view to judicial work. There is a discrete number appointed as "People's Peers", recommended by the general public. Twenty-six Church of England bishops have a seat in the House of Lords.

As a life peerage is not technically an "honour under the Crown", it cannot normally be withdrawn once granted. Thus, while knighthoods have been withdrawn as "honours under the Crown", convicted criminals who have served their sentences have returned to the House of Lords. In the case of [Lord Archer of Weston-super-Mare](/wiki/Jeffrey_Archer "Jeffrey Archer"), he has chosen only to exercise dining rights and has not spoken in Parliament since released from his perjury conviction.

### Baronetcy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Baronetcy")]

A [baronetcy](/wiki/Baronet "Baronet") is the lowest hereditary title in the United Kingdom. It carries the title [sir](/wiki/Sir "Sir"). In order of precedence, a Baronetcy is below a Barony but above most knighthoods.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Baronetcies are not peerages. When a baronetcy becomes vacant on the holder's death, the heir is required to register the proofs of succession if he wishes to be addressed as "Sir". The Official Roll of Baronets is kept at the Ministry of Justice, transferred from the Home Office in 2001, by the Registrar of the Baronetage. Anyone who considers that he is entitled to be entered on the roll may petition the Crown through the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"). Anyone succeeding to a baronetcy must exhibit proofs of succession to the Lord Chancellor.[[24]](#cite_note-24) A person who is not entered on the roll will not be addressed or mentioned as a baronet or accorded precedence as a baronet, effectively declining the honour. The baronetcy can be revived at any time on provision of acceptable proofs of succession.[[25]](#cite_note-25) As of 2017, 208 baronetcies are listed as presumedly not extinct but awaiting proofs of succession.[[26]](#cite_note-Baronetage-26)

As with hereditary peerages, baronetcies generally ceased to be granted after the Labour Party came to power in 1964. The sole subsequent exception was a baronetcy created in 1990 for the husband of [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"), Sir [Denis Thatcher](/wiki/Denis_Thatcher "Denis Thatcher"), later inherited by their son [Mark Thatcher](/wiki/Mark_Thatcher "Mark Thatcher").

### Knighthood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Knighthood")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Knight-Bachelor.ribbon.png/120px-Knight-Bachelor.ribbon.png)](/wiki/File:Knight-Bachelor.ribbon.png)

Ribbon of a Knight Bachelor

Descended from medieval [chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry"), knights exist both within the [orders of chivalry](/wiki/Order_of_chivalry "Order of chivalry") and in a class known as *[Knights Bachelor](/wiki/Knight_Bachelor "Knight Bachelor")*. Regular recipients include [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") judges and, to a lesser extent, [Chief Constables](/wiki/Chief_constable "Chief constable") of larger police forces. Knighthood carries the title *Sir*; the female equivalent *Dame* exists only within the orders of chivalry—Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire (DBE) is usually awarded as an equivalent of a Knight Bachelor.

### Order of St John

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Order of St John")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Order_of_St_John_%28UK%29_ribbon_-vector.svg/120px-Order_of_St_John_%28UK%29_ribbon_-vector.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Order_of_St_John_(UK)_ribbon_-vector.svg)

Ribbon of the Order of St John

The [Most Venerable Order of the Hospital of St John of Jerusalem](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_John_(chartered_1888) "Order of Saint John (chartered 1888)") established in 1888 with a royal charter, is a royal order of chivalry dedicated to charitable work, notably through St John Ambulance. Operating independently of the UK government under the patronage of the monarch as Sovereign Head, the Order’s ranks include Bailiff or Dame Grand Cross (GCStJ), Knight or Dame of Justice or Grace (KStJ/DStJ), Commander (CStJ), Officer (OStJ), and Member (MStJ). Members may wear the Order’s insignia, including those of Bailiff or Dame Grand Cross, as recognized in the UK order of wear.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) However, these ranks do not confer official rank in the order of precedence, and [post-nominal initials](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") (e.g., GCStJ, KStJ) are strictly unofficial and should never be used outside the Order's internal correspondence. Recipients of the highest grades are not addressed as “Sir” or “Dame”.

## British honours in the Commonwealth realms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: British honours in the Commonwealth realms")]

Main article: [Orders and decorations of the Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Orders_and_decorations_of_the_Commonwealth_realms "Orders and decorations of the Commonwealth realms")

Until the mid-20th century, the British honours system was the primary—and generally the sole—honours system utilised across the various [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") and territories of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), which became the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") from 1949. Today, British honours and decorations continue to be awarded to citizens of [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") and [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"). Within the Commonwealth realms, however, the nature of the British honour or decoration awarded and the permissibility of its conferment varies from government to government. The British honours system comprises both [dynastic honours](/wiki/Dynastic_orders_of_knighthood "Dynastic orders of knighthood"), the personal gift of the Sovereign, and British state honours or decorations (known as imperial honours or decorations outside the UK), which are not. Dynastic honours continue to be conferred by the Sovereign across the Commonwealth realms, although outside the United Kingdom they are typically non-titular[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] honours, such as the Order of Merit or the lower grades of the Royal Victorian Order.

In 1917 and 1919, the Canadian House of Commons passed the [Nickle Resolutions](/wiki/Canadian_titles_debate#The_Nickle_Resolution "Canadian titles debate"), which although non-binding gradually ended the conferment of titular honours—peerages, baronetcies, and knighthoods—to Canadians. Occasional conferments of knighthoods (in 1934 and 1935) and imperial honours, notably after the Second World War, continued until 1955, when the Canadian government officially ended all awards of imperial honours to Canadians.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In 1967, Canada established its own honours system with the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada"), created its own system of bravery decorations in 1972, and its own system of military decorations for valour in 1993. Canadian service personnel remained eligible to receive the imperial [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") until 1993, when a Canadian version was instituted. Canadian citizens remain eligible for imperial honours, including peerages and knighthoods, presumably for achievements within Great Britain, however such conferment may involve individual approval by the Canadian government.

In 1975, the Australian government established its own honours system, creating the [Order of Australia](/wiki/Order_of_Australia "Order of Australia") and its own system of bravery decorations. Indigenous police and fire service decorations followed from 1986, with a new system of military decorations created in 1991. Imperial honours continued to be conferred on Australians through 1989, when the last recommendations were made. With effect from 5 October 1992, the Australian government discontinued the awarding of imperial honours.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1975, [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") also instituted its first indigenous honour, the [Queen's Service Order](/wiki/Queen%27s_Service_Order "Queen's Service Order"), followed by the [Order of New Zealand](/wiki/Order_of_New_Zealand "Order of New Zealand") in 1987. In 1996, it replaced imperial honours with the [New Zealand Order of Merit](/wiki/New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "New Zealand Order of Merit"), and replaced imperial gallantry and bravery decorations with New Zealand decorations in 1999.

Other Commonwealth realms have continued to apply the imperial honours system together with their own honours systems. [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") established its own honours system in 1969, passing the *National Honours and Awards Act.* [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia") followed in 1980; the [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Solomon_Islands "Solomon Islands") in 1981; [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize") in 1991; [Antigua and Barbuda](/wiki/Antigua_and_Barbuda "Antigua and Barbuda") in 1998; [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea") in 2004; and [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada") in 2007. The government of the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas") continues to solely use imperial honours. In 2007, it passed a National Honours Act establishing a Bahamian honours system; however, it had not come into effect as of 2015.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In practice, legislation across the Commonwealth realms regulating the awarding of imperial honours to citizens of a realm, including knighthoods and damehoods, does not necessarily prevent a citizen of a Commonwealth realm from receiving a substantive award of an imperial honour for service in the United Kingdom or to its government. There continue to be numerous examples of Canadians, New Zealanders, and Australians who the British government have honored and can use the honour or its accompanying style when they are resident in their own nation or the United Kingdom.

## Honorary awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Honorary awards")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of honorary British knights and dames](/wiki/List_of_honorary_British_knights_and_dames "List of honorary British knights and dames").

Citizens of countries that do not have the King as their [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") sometimes have honours conferred upon them, in which case the awards are "honorary". In the case of knighthoods, the holders are entitled to place initials after their name but not style themselves "Sir" as they are not entitled to receive the [accolade](/wiki/Accolade "Accolade").[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Examples of foreigners with honorary knighthoods are [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham"), [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates"), [Bob Geldof](/wiki/Bob_Geldof "Bob Geldof"), [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono"), [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg"), [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams "John Williams") and [Rudolph Giuliani](/wiki/Rudolph_Giuliani "Rudolph Giuliani"), while [Arsène Wenger](/wiki/Ars%C3%A8ne_Wenger "Arsène Wenger") and [Gérard Houllier](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Houllier "Gérard Houllier") are honorary OBEs.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) Honorary knighthoods are of Orders of Chivalry rather than as Knights Bachelor as the latter confers no postnominal letters.

Recipients of honorary awards who later become subjects of His Majesty may apply to convert their awards to substantive ones.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Examples of this are [Marjorie Scardino](/wiki/Marjorie_Scardino "Marjorie Scardino"), American CEO of [Pearson PLC](/wiki/Pearson_PLC "Pearson PLC"), and [Yehudi Menuhin](/wiki/Yehudi_Menuhin "Yehudi Menuhin"), the American-born violinist and conductor. They were granted an honorary damehood and knighthood respectively while still American citizens, and converted them to substantive awards after they assumed British nationality, becoming Dame Marjorie and Sir Yehudi. Menuhin later accepted a life peerage with the title Lord Menuhin.

Sir [Tony O'Reilly](/wiki/Tony_O%27Reilly "Tony O'Reilly"), who holds both [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Irish](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") [nationality](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-subject-38) uses the style "Sir", but has also gained approval from the [Irish Government](/wiki/Irish_Government "Irish Government") to accept the award as is necessary under the [Irish Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland"). [Elisabeth Schwarzkopf](/wiki/Elisabeth_Schwarzkopf "Elisabeth Schwarzkopf"), the German soprano, became entitled to be known as "Dame Elisabeth" when she took British nationality. Irish-born Sir [Terry Wogan](/wiki/Terry_Wogan "Terry Wogan") was initially awarded an honorary knighthood, but by the time he collected the accolade from the Queen in December 2005, he had obtained dual nationality[[note 1]](#cite_note-subject-38) and the award was upgraded to a substantive knighthood.

[Bob Geldof](/wiki/Bob_Geldof "Bob Geldof") is often erroneously referred to as "Sir Bob"; he is not entitled to this style as an honorary knight, as he is a citizen of the Republic of Ireland (i.e., he is not a citizen of a Commonwealth realm).

There is no law in the UK preventing foreigners from holding a peerage (e.g., [Newburgh](/wiki/Earl_of_Newburgh "Earl of Newburgh")), though only [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and Irish citizens may sit in the House of Lords. This has yet to be tested under the new arrangements. However, some other countries have laws restricting the acceptances of awards granted to would-be recipients by foreign powers. In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), where the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") (but not the [Senate of Canada](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada")) has opposed the granting of titular honours with its (non-binding) [Nickle Resolution](/wiki/Nickle_Resolution "Nickle Resolution"), then Prime Minister [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien") advised the Queen not to grant [Conrad Black](/wiki/Conrad_Black "Conrad Black") a titular honour while he remained a Canadian citizen[[38]](#cite_note-39) (see [*Black v Chrétien*](/wiki/Black_v_Chr%C3%A9tien#Court_decision "Black v Chrétien")).

## Ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ceremony")]

Each year, around 2,600 people receive their awards personally from the monarch or another member of the [Royal Family](/wiki/Royal_Family "Royal Family"). The majority of [investitures](/wiki/Investiture "Investiture") take place at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), but an annual ceremony also takes place at the [Palace of Holyroodhouse](/wiki/Palace_of_Holyroodhouse "Palace of Holyroodhouse") in Edinburgh (during [Holyrood Week](/w/index.php?title=Holyrood_Week&action=edit&redlink=1 "Holyrood Week (page does not exist)")), and some happen at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"). There are approximately 120 recipients at each Investiture. In recent years the [King](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales") and [The Princess Royal](/wiki/Anne,_Princess_Royal "Anne, Princess Royal") have all held investitures.

During the ceremony, the monarch enters the ballroom of [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") attended by two [Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") orderly officers, a tradition begun in 1876 by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"). On duty on the dais are five members of the King's Body Guard of the [Yeomen of the Guard](/wiki/Yeomen_of_the_Guard "Yeomen of the Guard"), which was created in 1485 by [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"); they are the oldest, but not most senior, military corps in the United Kingdom. Three [Lady or Gentleman Ushers](/wiki/Lady_and_Gentleman_Usher "Lady and Gentleman Usher") are on duty to help look after the recipients and their guests.

The King or his representative is escorted by either the [Lord Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Chamberlain "Lord Chamberlain") or the [Lord Steward](/wiki/Lord_Steward "Lord Steward"). After the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") has been played, he stands to the right of the King and announces the name of each recipient and the achievement for which they are being decorated. The King or his representative is provided with a brief background for each recipient by their [equerry](/wiki/Equerry "Equerry") as they approach to receive their decorations.

Men who are to be [knighted](/wiki/Knight "Knight") kneel on an investiture stool to receive the [accolade](/wiki/Accolade "Accolade"), which the King bestows. [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") used the sword used by her father, [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") as [Duke of York](/wiki/Duke_of_York "Duke of York") and [Colonel](/wiki/Colonel_(United_Kingdom) "Colonel (United Kingdom)") of the [Scots Guards](/wiki/Scots_Guards "Scots Guards"). Only men are knighted. Women receive their honours in the same fashion as men receiving decorations or medals, even if they are receiving a damehood. Occasionally an award for gallantry may be made posthumously and in this case, the King or his representative presents the decoration or medal to the recipient's next-of-kin in private before the public [investiture](/wiki/Investiture "Investiture") begins. The [Elizabeth Cross](/wiki/Elizabeth_Cross "Elizabeth Cross") was created especially for this purpose.

After the investiture ceremony, those honoured are ushered out of the ballroom into the Inner Quadrangle of Buckingham Palace, where the royal rota's photographers are stationed. Here recipients are photographed with their awards. In some cases, members of the press may interview some of the more well-known people who have been honoured.

## Refusal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Refusal")]

See also: [List of people who have declined a British honour](/wiki/List_of_people_who_have_declined_a_British_honour "List of people who have declined a British honour")

In 2003, *[Sunday Times](/wiki/Sunday_Times "Sunday Times")* published a list of almost 300 people who had declined an honour between 1951 and 1999. In 2020, *[the Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* reported based on a [Freedom of Information](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information "Freedom of Information") request, that the number of people refusing an honour had more than doubled in the previous nine years.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### Notable examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Notable examples")]

* Outgoing prime ministers:
  + [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") declined an [earldom](/wiki/Earl "Earl") and appointment as [Knight Companion of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter").
  + Sir [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") declined a [dukedom](/wiki/Duke "Duke") in order to remain in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") and to allow his son [Randolph Churchill](/wiki/Randolph_Churchill "Randolph Churchill") a political career.
  + Sir [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath") declined a [life peerage](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer") in order to remain in the House of Commons.
  + Sir [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") declined a life peerage, saying elevation to the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") was incompatible with retirement from politics.
  + Sir [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") declined a life peerage.
  + [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") declined a life peerage.
* [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"), novelist
* [John Cleese](/wiki/John_Cleese "John Cleese") declined appointment as CBE and later declined a life peerage.
* [Frank Pick](/wiki/Frank_Pick "Frank Pick") declined both a knighthood and a peerage.
* [T. E. Lawrence](/wiki/T._E._Lawrence "T. E. Lawrence") declined a knighthood, as he felt that Britain had betrayed the Arabs.
* [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") declined appointment as CBE in 2000, then declined a knighthood in 2003, stating, "It's not what I spent my life working for."[[40]](#cite_note-41)
* [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene "Graham Greene"), novelist
* [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), physicist, reportedly turned down a knighthood because he "does not like titles."[[41]](#cite_note-42)
* [Benjamin Zephaniah](/wiki/Benjamin_Zephaniah "Benjamin Zephaniah") declined appointment as OBE, describing it as a legacy of colonialism.[[42]](#cite_note-43)
* [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") declined appointment as OM, replying that "merit" in authorship could only be determined by the posthumous verdict of history.
* [Honor Blackman](/wiki/Honor_Blackman "Honor Blackman") declined appointment as CBE, as she was a republican.
* [Albert Finney](/wiki/Albert_Finney "Albert Finney"), actor (in 2000; had previously declined appointment as CBE in 1980).[[43]](#cite_note-Guardian2003-44)
* [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") returned his MBE (awarded in 1965) in 1969, although no official provision exists for renouncing an honour.[[44]](#cite_note-45)
* [Geraldine McEwan](/wiki/Geraldine_McEwan "Geraldine McEwan") declined a damehood.
* [Vanessa Redgrave](/wiki/Vanessa_Redgrave "Vanessa Redgrave") declined a damehood in 1999, but accepted it in 2022. She also accepted appointment as CBE in 1967.[[43]](#cite_note-Guardian2003-44)
* [Gareth Peirce](/wiki/Gareth_Peirce "Gareth Peirce") declined appointment as CBE.
* [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") resigned his knighthood in protest against the [Jallianwala Bagh](/wiki/Jallianwala_Bagh_massacre "Jallianwala Bagh massacre") - one of the bloodiest massacres in Indian and British histories.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

## Revocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Revocation")]

See also: [List of revocations of appointments to orders and awarded decorations and medals of the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_revocations_of_appointments_to_orders_and_awarded_decorations_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of revocations of appointments to orders and awarded decorations and medals of the United Kingdom")

Honours are sometimes [revoked](/wiki/Revocation "Revocation") (forfeited), for example if a recipient is subsequently convicted of a serious [criminal offence](/wiki/Criminal_offence "Criminal offence"). The [Honours Forfeiture Committee](/wiki/Honours_Forfeiture_Committee "Honours Forfeiture Committee") is an *ad hoc* committee convened under the chairmanship of the [Head of the Home Civil Service](/wiki/Head_of_the_Home_Civil_Service "Head of the Home Civil Service"),[[46]](#cite_note-47) to consider cases where information has been received which indicates an individual is unsuitable to hold an award. Sometimes the original decision to grant an honour was made on the basis of inaccurate information (including through manipulation of the public nominations system), but normally cases relate to actions that took place after the award was made. Recommendations are made to the [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), who has the sole authority to rescind an honour.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In 2009, [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") confirmed[[48]](#cite_note-hansard-1-49) that the process remains as set out in 1994 by the then Prime Minister [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") in a written answer to the House of Commons:

> The statutes of most orders of knighthood and the royal warrants of decorations and medals include provision for the Queen to "cancel and annul" appointments and awards. Cancellation is considered in cases where retention of the appointment or award would bring the honours system into disrepute. There are no set guidelines for cancellations, which are considered on a case-by-case basis. Since 1979, the *[London Gazette](/wiki/London_Gazette "London Gazette")* has published details of cancellations of 15 appointments and awards—three knighthoods, one CBE, five OBEs, four MBEs and two BEMs.[[49]](#cite_note-hansard-2-50)

In October 2016, the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") approved a motion to ask the Honours Forfeiture Committee to strip Sir [Philip Green](/wiki/Philip_Green "Philip Green") of his knighthood for his role in the downfall of [British Home Stores](/wiki/British_Home_Stores "British Home Stores"). It was the first time MPs voted to recommend rescinding a knighthood.[[50]](#cite_note-dt102016-51)

### Notable examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Notable examples")]

* [Kaiser Wilhelm II](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_II "Kaiser Wilhelm II") was a [Knight Companion of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter") prior to the start of the First World War, when it was revoked.
* [Roger Casement](/wiki/Roger_Casement "Roger Casement") had been honoured for his human rights work while a British diplomat; however, upon being convicted on 30 June 1916 of [High Treason](/wiki/Treason_Act_1351 "Treason Act 1351") for his role in the Irish [1916 Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising"), the King directed that Casement would cease to be a member of the Order of St Michael and St George, his name being stricken from the register, and [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") were also issued "degrading" him "from the degree, title and dignity of Knight Bachelor".[[51]](#cite_note-52) He was executed that August.
* [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") was stripped of his honorary knighthood after Italy declared war on the United Kingdom in 1940.
* [Anthony Blunt](/wiki/Anthony_Blunt "Anthony Blunt"), knighted as Surveyor of the Queen's Pictures for his services to Art, lost his knighthood in the 1980s when he was revealed to be the "Fourth Man" in the early 1950s [Burgess and Maclean spying scandal](/wiki/Burgess-Maclean_spy_affair "Burgess-Maclean spy affair") which also touched on the 1960s [Philby](/wiki/Kim_Philby "Kim Philby") spying affair, as a result of which he confessed to the security services. Although Blunt was never charged or convicted, the honour was withdrawn on the advice of the then Prime Minister, Margaret Thatcher.
* [Nicolae Ceaușescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu "Nicolae Ceaușescu") forfeited his honorary knighthood that he earlier received for his friendship with Western democracies.
* [Albert Henry](/wiki/Albert_Henry_(politician) "Albert Henry (politician)") was the former Premier of the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands"). He was later convicted of [electoral fraud](/wiki/Electoral_fraud "Electoral fraud") in the 1980s.
* [Terry Lewis](/wiki/Terry_Lewis_(police_officer) "Terry Lewis (police officer)"), knighted for his services to [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") police, was stripped of his knighthood in 1993 after being sentenced to prison on charges of corruption and forgery as a result of the findings of the [Fitzgerald Inquiry](/wiki/Fitzgerald_Inquiry "Fitzgerald Inquiry").
* [Jack Lyons](/wiki/Jack_Lyons_(financier) "Jack Lyons (financier)"), who had received his knighthood for his huge charitable donations and services to industry, lost it when he was convicted of fraud in the 1980s.
* [Robert Mugabe](/wiki/Robert_Mugabe "Robert Mugabe") was stripped of his honorary [GCB](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath") after calls from the Government to the Royal Family due to the crisis in [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") under his régime, including [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation"), and violence in the 2008 presidential elections.
* [Lester Piggott](/wiki/Lester_Piggott "Lester Piggott"), the high-profile jockey, had his [OBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") removed after being convicted and sentenced to imprisonment for tax evasion.
* [Fred Goodwin](/wiki/Fred_Goodwin "Fred Goodwin"), the former CEO of [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland"), had his knighthood annulled, after the [Financial Services Authority](/wiki/Financial_Services_Authority "Financial Services Authority") found that the failure of RBS had an important role in the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), because Goodwin was the dominant decision maker at the time.
* [James Crosby](/wiki/James_Crosby_(banker) "James Crosby (banker)"), former CEO of [HBOS](/wiki/HBOS "HBOS") and non-executive director of the [Financial Services Authority](/wiki/Financial_Services_Authority "Financial Services Authority"), was stripped of his knighthood at his request in April 2013 as the result of the bank's collapse during the UK financial crisis.
* [Ronald Brierley](/wiki/Ronald_Brierley "Ronald Brierley"), New Zealand investor and corporate raider, chairman and director of a number of companies in Australia, New Zealand and the UK. In April 2021, Brierley pleaded guilty to three counts of possessing child abuse material and resigned his 1988 knighthood after the New Zealand government had initiated the process of having it removed.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)

## Order of Wear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Order of Wear")]

Main article: [United Kingdom Honours Order of Wearing](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Honours_Order_of_Wearing "United Kingdom Honours Order of Wearing")

Honours, decorations and medals are arranged in the "[Order of Wear](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Honours_Order_of_Wearing "United Kingdom Honours Order of Wearing")", an official list which describes the order in which they should be worn.[[54]](#cite_note-55) Updates to the Order of Wear are published in *[The London Gazette](/wiki/The_London_Gazette "The London Gazette")* when necessary. The current Order of Wear was published on 11 January 2019.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Additional information on the social events at which an award may be worn is contained in the insignia case given to each recipient.

The list places the Victoria Cross and George Cross at the top, followed by the orders of knighthood arranged in order of date of creation. Below the Knights of the Garter and Thistle, individuals of a higher rank precede those of a lower rank. For instance, a Knight Grand Cross of any order precedes any Knight Commander. For those of equal rank, members of the higher-ranked Order take precedence. Within the same Order, precedence is accorded to that individual who received the honour earlier.

Not all orders have the same number of ranks. The Order of Merit, the Order of the Companions of Honour, the Distinguished Service Order and the Imperial Service Order are slightly different, being single-rank honours, and have been placed at appropriate positions of seniority. The precedence of Knight Bachelor is below the knights of the different orders and above those with the rank of Commander or lower.

Decorations are followed by medals of various categories, being arranged in date order within each section. These are followed by [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and honorary foreign awards of any level. Miscellaneous details are explained in notes at the bottom of the list.

The order of wear is not connected to and should not be confused with the [order of precedence](/wiki/Order_of_precedence "Order of precedence").

## Style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Style")]

For peers, see [forms of address in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Forms_of_address_in_the_United_Kingdom "Forms of address in the United Kingdom").

For baronets, the style *Sir John Smith, Bt* (or *Bart*) is used. Their wives are styled *Lady Smith*. A baronetess is styled *Dame Jane Smith, Btss*.

For knights, the style *Sir John Smith [[postnominals](/wiki/List_of_post-nominal_letters "List of post-nominal letters")]* is used, attaching the proper postnominal letters depending on rank and order (for knights bachelor, no postnominal letters are used). Their wives are styled *Lady Smith*, with no postnominal letters. A dame is styled *Dame Jane Smith, [postnominals]*. More familiar references or oral addresses use the first name only, e.g. *Sir John*, or *Dame Joan*.

Wives of knights and baronets are styled *Lady Smith*, although customarily no courtesy title is automatically reciprocated to male consorts.

Recipients of [orders, decorations and medals](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_Commonwealth_realms "Orders, decorations, and medals of the Commonwealth realms") receive no styling of *Sir* or *Dame*, but they may attach the according postnominal letters to their name, e.g., *John Smith, VC*. Recipients of gallantry awards may be referred to in Parliament as "gallant", in addition to "honourable", "noble", etc.: *The honourable and gallant Gentleman*.

Bailiffs or Dames Grand Cross (GCStJ), Knights/Dames of Justice/Grace (KStJ/DStJ), Commander Brothers/Sisters (CStJ), Officer Brothers/Sisters (OStJ), Serving Brothers/Sisters (SBStJ/SSStJ), and Esquires (EsqStJ) of the Order of St John do not receive any special styling with regards to prenominal address i.e. Sir or Dame. They may, however, attach the relevant postnominal initials (solely) within internal correspondence of the Order[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. In the Priory of Australia, Canada and the United States, the rank of Serving Brother/Sister is no longer granted. The rank now awarded is referred to as Member of the Order of St John for both men and women.

For honours bestowed upon those in the [entertainment industry](/wiki/Entertainment_industry "Entertainment industry") (e.g., [Anthony Hopkins](/wiki/Anthony_Hopkins "Anthony Hopkins"), [Maggie Smith](/wiki/Maggie_Smith "Maggie Smith")), it is an accepted practice to omit the title for professional credits.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

## Reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Reform")]

See also: [1993 reviews of the British honours system](/wiki/1993_reviews_of_the_British_honours_system "1993 reviews of the British honours system")

Reforms of the system occur from time to time. In the last century notable changes to the system have included a Royal Commission in 1925 following the scandal in which Prime Minister [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") was found to be selling honours. The sale of British Honours, including titles, is now prohibited by the [Honours (Prevention of Abuses) Act 1925](/wiki/Honours_(Prevention_of_Abuses)_Act_1925 "Honours (Prevention of Abuses) Act 1925").

A [significant set of reforms](/wiki/1993_reviews_of_the_British_honours_system "1993 reviews of the British honours system") were enacted in 1993 under the Conservative Prime Minister [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major"), with the intention of providing a more merits-based system for honouring service and achievement.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Among other outcomes, this removed the rank distinctions that were explicitly linked to certain military awards, and ended (with the exception of High Court Judges) the awarding of *ex officio* honours. The reforms also provided for people to be nominated for an honour by members of the public, as well as placing more emphasis within the system on recognising and rewarding voluntary service in particular.[[58]](#cite_note-malloch-59)

In July 2004, the [Public Administration Select Committee](/wiki/List_of_Committees_of_the_United_Kingdom_Parliament "List of Committees of the United Kingdom Parliament") (PASC) of the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") and, concurrently, Sir [Hayden Phillips](/wiki/Hayden_Phillips "Hayden Phillips"), [Permanent Secretary](/wiki/Permanent_Secretary "Permanent Secretary") at the [Department of Constitutional Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Constitutional_Affairs "Department of Constitutional Affairs"), both concluded reviews of the system. The PASC recommended some radical changes; Sir Hayden concentrated on issues of procedure and transparency. In February 2005, the Government responded to both reviews by issuing a [Command paper](/wiki/Command_paper "Command paper") detailing which of the proposed changes it had accepted. These included diversifying and opening up the system of [honours selection committees](/wiki/Honours_Committee "Honours Committee") for the Prime Minister's list and also the introduction of a miniature badge. Furthermore, the 2004 review suggested a regular report on the transparency and operation of the system at a suggested frequency of every 3 years. These reviews have taken place in [2008](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/operation-of-the-honours-system-2008), [2011](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/61213/Second_report_on_operation_of_the_reformed_honours_system.pdf), [2014](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/operation-of-the-honours-system-2014), [2018](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/807228/Report-on-the-Operation-of-the-Honours-System-Final-for-Publication-1.pdf) and [2023](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/operation-of-the-honours-system-2023/the-fifth-report-on-the-operation-of-the-honours-system-html). The most recent review covers the period 2019 to 2023 and shows a notable shift towards community and voluntary recognition, and a continued emphasis on ensuring that the Honours System reflects British societal diversity.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

As of 2012, same-sex [civil partners](/wiki/Civil_partnership_in_the_United_Kingdom "Civil partnership in the United Kingdom") of individual recipients of British honours are not themselves granted honours by way of their partnered relation. In July 2012, Conservative MP [Oliver Colvile](/wiki/Oliver_Colvile "Oliver Colvile") introduced a [private member's bill](/wiki/Private_member%27s_bill "Private member's bill"), titled "Honours (Equality of Titles for Partners) Bill", to amend the honours system to both allow husbands of those made [dames](/wiki/Dame_(title) "Dame (title)") and for civil partners of recipients to receive honours by their relationship statuses.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

In May 2020, a special COVID nomination process was introduced to provide a more streamlined consideration process and accessible route for the public to nominate those in society who had responded to the COVID-19 pandemic. Compared with the existing public nomination form, it required substantially less information in order to be accepted and processed. This process was ended in May 2022.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Controversies")]

A scandal in the 1920s was the sale by [Maundy Gregory](/wiki/Maundy_Gregory "Maundy Gregory") of honours and peerages to raise political funds for [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George").

In 1976, the [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") era was mired by controversy over the [1976 Prime Minister's Resignation Honours](/wiki/1976_Prime_Minister%27s_Resignation_Honours "1976 Prime Minister's Resignation Honours"), which became known as the "Lavender List".

In 2006, *The Sunday Times* newspaper revealed that every donor who had given £1,000,000 or more to the Labour Party since 1997 was given a Knighthood or a Peerage (see [Cash-for-Honours scandal](/wiki/Cash-for-Honours_scandal "Cash-for-Honours scandal")). Moreover, the government had given honours to 12 of the 14 individuals who have donated more than £200,000 to Labour and of the 22 who donated more than £100,000, 17 received honours. An investigation by the Crown Prosecution Service did not lead to any charges being made.

*The Times* published an analysis of the recipients of honours in December 2015 which showed that 46% of those getting knighthoods and above in 2015 had been to fee-paying [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)"). In 1955 it was 50%. Only 6.55% of the population attends such schools. 27% had been to Oxford or Cambridge universities (18% in 1955).[[62]](#cite_note-63)
